content.text: What Is Autonomy Level?
An agent's autonomy level is
how much it can act without human approval

. A fully manual system executes nothing without explicit human confirmation at every step. A fully autonomous system decides and acts on its own, only surfacing results when the task is complete. Most production agents sit somewhere in between.
Autonomy level is not a property of the LLM — it is a property of
the host application code

. The same model can power a level-1 assistant (proposes only) or a level-4 agent (acts freely) depending on which approval gates the application enforces. Choosing the right level is an engineering decision, not a model capability question.
The fundamental tradeoff is
speed and throughput vs. oversight and safety
. Higher autonomy means fewer human bottlenecks and faster task completion. Lower autonomy means more checkpoints and more opportunities to catch errors before they propagate into real consequences.
The Autonomy Spectrum
Five autonomy levels cover most real-world agent deployments:
Level
Name
Who approves actions?
Typical use case
Human approves every action before execution
Sensitive, irreversible tasks; initial deployment of a new agent
Human approves high-risk actions; routine ones auto-execute
Customer support: auto-close tickets, flag refunds for approval
Agent acts freely; human can review and override after the fact
Data enrichment pipelines; non-financial SaaS automation
Agent acts freely within defined constraints; pauses at boundaries
Financial agent: auto-execute orders under $1,000; escalate above
Agent acts and reports; human sees results, not decisions
Fully trusted internal tooling; mature, high-coverage tested agents
Levels 0 and 1 are appropriate during
initial deployment

: before you have data on the agent's error rate in production. Levels 2 and 3 are the most common production configurations. Level 4 is reserved for agents with a long track record of correct behavior on a well-bounded task set.
Selecting the Right Level
Three criteria drive the decision:
Action reversibility

: can the action be undone? Irreversible actions (send email, delete record, charge card, deploy code) require approval at a lower level than reversible ones (read record, draft email, stage deployment).
Blast radius

: if the agent makes an error, how many records, users, or dollars are affected? High blast radius (batch operations, financial transactions) demands lower autonomy. Low blast radius (single record reads, informational lookups) tolerates higher autonomy.
Track record

: what is the agent's error rate on this task type? New tasks start at level 0–1. After 500+ successful runs with no errors, promote to level 2. After a sustained error rate below 0.5% across thousands of runs, consider level 3.

from enum import IntEnum
from dataclasses import dataclass

class AutonomyLevel(IntEnum):
    MANUAL = 0          # every action requires approval
    SUPERVISED = 1      # only high-risk actions require approval
    ON_THE_LOOP = 2     # agent acts freely; audit log only
    CONDITIONAL = 3     # acts freely within constraints; pauses at boundary
    FULL = 4            # acts and reports; no human gate

@dataclass
class ActionPolicy:
    """Defines what counts as high-risk for a given autonomy level."""
    requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1: bool  # always requires approval up to level 1
    constraint_check: callable | None = None  # for level 3: returns True if within bounds

# Each action type has a policy
ACTION_POLICIES: dict[str, ActionPolicy] = {
    "read_record":    ActionPolicy(requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=False),
    "draft_email":    ActionPolicy(requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=False),
    "update_record":  ActionPolicy(requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=True),
    "send_email":     ActionPolicy(requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=True),
    "charge_card":    ActionPolicy(
        requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=True,
        constraint_check=lambda args: args.get("amount_usd", 0) <= 1000,
    ),
    "delete_record":  ActionPolicy(requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1=True),
}

def requires_human_approval(
    action: str,
    args: dict,
    level: AutonomyLevel,
) -> bool:
    """
    True if this action needs human approval at the given autonomy level.
    """
    policy = ACTION_POLICIES.get(action)
    if policy is None:
        # Unknown action: always require approval
        return True

    if level == AutonomyLevel.FULL:
        return False  # never pause

    if level <= AutonomyLevel.SUPERVISED:
        # Level 0: everything requires approval
        # Level 1: high-risk actions require approval
        if level == AutonomyLevel.MANUAL:
            return True
        return policy.requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1

    if level == AutonomyLevel.ON_THE_LOOP:
        return False  # act freely, log for review

    if level == AutonomyLevel.CONDITIONAL:
        # Pause if the constraint check fails (or no constraint defined for this action)
        if policy.constraint_check is None:
            return policy.requires_approval_at_level_0_and_1
        return not policy.constraint_check(args)

    return False

# Example: test requires_human_approval at each level
test_cases = [
    ("read_record",   {"id": 42},             "Should never block"),
    ("send_email",    {"to": "user@example.com"}, "Should block at 0, 1"),
    ("charge_card",   {"amount_usd": 500},    "Should block at 0, 1; pass at 3 (<=1000)"),
    ("charge_card",   {"amount_usd": 2000},   "Should block at 0, 1, 3 (>1000)"),
]
for action, args, note in test_cases:
    results = {lvl.name: requires_human_approval(action, args, lvl)
               for lvl in AutonomyLevel}
    print(f"{action}({args}) — {note}")
    print(f"  {results}")
The
ACTION_POLICIES
dictionary is the
single source of truth
for what constitutes a high-risk action. The
requires_human_approval
function is called by the host code before every tool dispatch. Adding a new tool means adding a new entry to
ACTION_POLICIES
— the autonomy control logic does not change.
The Approval Gate Pattern

At levels 0–1 and at boundaries in level 3, the agent must pause for human approval. The approval gate intercepts the tool call, presents it to a human (or an automated policy check), and returns the verdict to the agent as a tool result.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def prompt_for_approval(action: str, args: dict) -> bool:
    """
    In production: present to a human via Slack/email/UI.
    Here: simulate with a yes/no prompt.
    """
    print(f"\n[APPROVAL REQUIRED] Action: {action}")
    print(f"  Arguments: {json.dumps(args, indent=2)}")
    answer = input("  Approve? (y/n): ").strip().lower()
    return answer == "y"

def dispatch_with_approval(
    action: str,
    args: dict,
    dispatch: dict,
    level: AutonomyLevel,
) -> str:
    """
    Run the action, pausing for human approval if required at this autonomy level.
    Returns the tool result string.
    """
    if requires_human_approval(action, args, level):
        approved = prompt_for_approval(action, args)
        if not approved:
            return json.dumps({
                "ok": False,
                "error": "Action rejected by human reviewer.",
                "action": action,
                "args": args,
            })

    fn = dispatch.get(action)
    if fn is None:
        return json.dumps({"ok": False, "error": f"Unknown action: {action}"})
    return fn(**args)

# Use dispatch_with_approval instead of fn(**block.input) in the agent loop:
#
#   for block in tool_calls:
#       result = dispatch_with_approval(
#           action=block.name,
#           args=block.input,
#           dispatch=DISPATCH,
#           level=AutonomyLevel.SUPERVISED,  # set per environment
#       )
#       results.append({"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": result})
In a web application,
prompt_for_approval
would open a Slack notification, a dashboard item, or an email — anything that gets a human to approve or reject. The agent loop is paused until the response arrives. The rejection result
{"ok": false, "error": "Action rejected"}
is returned to the agent as a normal tool result, so the agent can adjust its plan (try a different action, or call
task_failed
The Minimal Footprint Principle
An agent should
request only the permissions it needs for the current task
and should acquire them
as late as possible

(just before use) rather than upfront. This is the agentic equivalent of the least-privilege security principle: an agent that holds a payment-processing credential for a task that only needs read access represents unnecessary risk.
Minimal footprint has three practical implications:
Scope-limited credentials
: pass API keys and tokens scoped to the minimum required capability. An agent that reads invoices should have a read-only token, not a full-access token.
Prefer reversible actions
: when two tools achieve the same result, choose the one that can be undone. Stage before deploy. Draft before send. Backup before delete.
Clean up after the task
: revoke temporary credentials, close open connections, and release locks as soon as they are no longer needed.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Minimal footprint: the agent is given only the tools it needs.
# A read-only research agent should not have write tools in its tool list.

READ_ONLY_TOOLS = [
    {"name": "get_invoice",
     "description": "Read a single invoice by ID.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"invoice_id": {"type": "string"}},
                      "required": ["invoice_id"]}},
    {"name": "list_invoices",
     "description": "List invoices for a given customer.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"customer_id": {"type": "string"},
                                     "status": {"type": "string",
                                                "enum": ["paid", "unpaid", "overdue"]}},
                      "required": ["customer_id"]}},
    {"name": "task_complete",
     "description": "Return the result of the lookup.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"summary": {"type": "string"}},
                      "required": ["summary"]}},
]

WRITE_TOOLS = [
    {"name": "update_invoice_status",
     "description": "Mark an invoice as paid or cancelled.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"invoice_id": {"type": "string"},
                                     "new_status": {"type": "string",
                                                    "enum": ["paid", "cancelled"]}},
                      "required": ["invoice_id", "new_status"]}},
]

def run_minimal_footprint_agent(task: str, allow_writes: bool = False) -> dict:
    """
    Pass only the tools this specific task requires.
    Write tools are excluded unless explicitly needed.
    """
    tools = READ_ONLY_TOOLS
    if allow_writes:
        tools = READ_ONLY_TOOLS + WRITE_TOOLS

    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    for _ in range(10):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system="You are an invoice assistant. Use only the tools provided.",
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})
        tool_calls = [b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        for block in tool_calls:
            if block.name == "task_complete":
                return {"status": "complete", "result": block.input["summary"]}
        if not tool_calls:
            return {"status": "stuck"}
        results = [{"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": b.id,
                    "content": json.dumps({"ok": False, "error": "stub"})}
                   for b in tool_calls]
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": results})
    return {"status": "max_iterations"}

# Read-only task: no write tools in the tool list
result_read = run_minimal_footprint_agent(
    "How many unpaid invoices does customer C-001 have?",
    allow_writes=False,
)

# Write task: write tools added only when needed
result_write = run_minimal_footprint_agent(
    "Mark invoice INV-99 as paid.",
    allow_writes=True,
)
Passing write tools to a read-only task is a
latent risk

: even if the agent would never call them unprompted, an adversarial prompt injection or a reasoning error could cause it to. The safest enforcement is not in the approval gate — it is in the tool list itself: a tool that is not offered cannot be called.
Graduated Autonomy: When to Promote
Autonomy level should increase gradually as evidence of reliability accumulates. Promoting too early amplifies undetected errors at production scale. The promotion decision should be driven by
metrics, not confidence
Promotion gate
Metric to measure
Suggested threshold before promoting
Error rate (human reviews corrections made)
< 2% error rate over 200+ consecutive reviewed actions
Error rate on auto-approved actions (no human in loop)
< 1% error rate over 500+ auto-approved actions; no catastrophic errors
Constraint violation rate (actions that would have exceeded limits)
< 0.5% constraint violations over 1,000+ actions; 0 large-blast-radius errors
Overall outcome correctness (spot-checked by humans)
< 0.2% error rate over 5,000+ actions; verified on at least 3 distinct task types
A
regression trigger

must accompany every promotion: if the error rate rises above 2x the promotion threshold after promotion, automatically demote to the previous level and alert the team. Autonomous systems accumulate errors silently — without a regression trigger, a promoted agent that starts making mistakes will continue until a human notices.
Audit Logging by Level
Each autonomy level requires a different depth of audit logging. At level 0 every human decision is already logged by the approval gate. At level 4 there is no approval gate — the audit log
is
the primary safety mechanism.
Autonomy level
Minimum log contents
Review cadence
Proposed action + arguments; human decision (approve/reject); timestamp
On-demand — review any rejected actions immediately
All executed actions + arguments + results; which actions were auto-approved vs. human-approved
Daily summary of auto-approved actions
All executed actions + arguments + results + latency; any tool errors
Weekly sample review; alert on error rate spike
All actions + constraint check outcome + which constraint triggered
Weekly review; immediate alert if constraint threshold is breached
All actions + arguments + results + model reasoning (if extractable); outcome verdict
Automated anomaly detection; human review of flagged runs
Audit logs serve two purposes:
incident investigation
when something goes wrong, and
promotion evidence

when deciding to increase autonomy. A log that only records success/failure is not enough — you also need the action arguments to diagnose why a failure occurred and the model's reasoning to understand what it intended. Include the model response's
stop_reason
, the tool call sequence, and the final output in every log entry.
Autonomy level is a host-side configuration, not a model property.

Set it based on action reversibility, blast radius, and track record. Start new agents at level 0 or 1 and promote them as you accumulate evidence of correct behavior in production. Use an ACTION_POLICIES dictionary as the single source of truth for which actions need approval at which level. The approval gate is a synchronous pause in the dispatch loop: the agent sees rejection as a tool result and adjusts its plan.
